---
title: "Module 10: Corporate AI Deployment"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---


## Data Security {.section-divider}

## The Agent Evaluation Loop

An AI agent that queries your database does not just send one message --- it runs a [multi-turn loop]{.amber}.

:::{.step-flow}
:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
1
:::
:::{.step-title}
Prompt
:::
:::{.step-desc}
System prompt + user question sent to LLM
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
2
:::
:::{.step-title}
Generate
:::
:::{.step-desc}
LLM writes SQL or Python
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
3
:::
:::{.step-title}
Execute
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Code runs locally against your database
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
4
:::
:::{.step-title}
Evaluate
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Results sent back to LLM for review
:::
:::
:::

Steps 1--3 can be made safe. Step 4 is where [real data may leak]{.amber} to the cloud provider --- query results, error messages containing data values, or raw rows the LLM needs to interpret.

## Protecting Step 1: Schema + Synthetic Rows

The system prompt should describe your database using [schema and synthetic rows]{.amber} --- never real data.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**What to include**

- Table names, column names, data types
- ~3 [synthetic]{.amber} rows per table (fabricated, matching real formats)
- Column descriptions and domain conventions
- Databricks: ~3 synthetic rows raises SQL accuracy by [~6 pp]{.amber}
::::

::::{.column}
**Why synthetic rows help**

- Reveal data formats (is status `'Active'` or `1`?)
- Disambiguate columns (is `code` a country code or product code?)
- Help the LLM write correct WHERE clauses
- [No real records are transmitted]{.amber}
::::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
Schema metadata is not PII --- safe to share. The LLM writes the query; your server runs it.
:::

## Protecting Step 4: The Hard Part

When the agent loop sends results back to the LLM, [real data leaves your network]{.amber}. Mitigations exist but each has gaps.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Mitigations**]{.card-title}

- **Aggregate only**: send row counts and summary statistics, never raw rows
- **Error messages only**: if SQL fails, send the error string (schema info, not data) for correction; if it succeeds, don't send results back
- **Redaction layer**: middleware that strips values matching PII patterns before sending to the LLM
:::

:::{.card .card-dark}
[**Why Mitigations Are Not Foolproof**]{.card-title}

- A query like *"show the account with the highest balance"* returns one row --- that [is]{.amber} PII
- SQL errors can echo offending data values
- Redaction is pattern-based and misses context-dependent sensitivity
- "Don't send results back" limits the agent to single-turn --- you lose the error-correction loop that makes agents useful
:::
:::

## Data Policies: Training and Retention

Even after mitigating the evaluation loop, broader provider policies determine what happens to data that does reach the cloud. Before sending any data to a cloud AI provider, know the answers to two questions.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Will they train on your data?**

- [Consumer tiers]{.amber} (free ChatGPT, free Claude) may use your input to improve models
- [API and enterprise tiers]{.amber} typically do not
- Check your agreement and settings
::::

::::{.column}
**How long do they keep it?**

- Providers retain prompts for a window (often 30 days) for safety monitoring
- Enterprise contracts may offer zero retention
- Know the policy [before]{.amber} you send sensitive data
::::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
Even with zero-training guarantees, data still [transits]{.amber} an external server. For regulated data, transit alone may violate compliance.
:::

## The Deployment Spectrum

For most organizations, the answer is not "all cloud" or "all on-prem" --- it is a [spectrum]{.amber}.

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Public Cloud API**]{.card-title}

- Research, drafting, coding assistance
- Non-sensitive data analysis
- Frontier model quality
- No infrastructure to manage
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Cloud-Hosted Private AI**]{.card-title}

- AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, GCP Vertex AI
- Models run in your VPC with private endpoints
- Customer-managed encryption keys
- Meets HIPAA, SOX, FedRAMP for many use cases
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**On-Prem / Private Cloud**]{.card-title}

- Air-gapped or classified environments
- Data that cannot transit any external network
- Full physical control of hardware
- Highest cost, highest data control
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
Cloud-hosted private AI (middle column) is the [most common enterprise pattern]{.amber} today. True on-prem is reserved for the most sensitive workloads.
:::

## Emerging: Confidential Computing

An emerging technology that may shift this spectrum: [confidential computing]{.amber} allows data to be encrypted even while being processed.

- **Intel SGX, AMD SEV, AWS Nitro Enclaves** --- hardware-level isolation where even the cloud provider cannot see the data
- If proven at scale, this could allow cloud AI to handle sensitive workloads without the evaluation-loop risk
- Still maturing --- not yet widely adopted for LLM inference

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
Watch this space. Confidential computing could collapse the middle of the spectrum into the left column.
:::

## Private Cloud: On-Prem Managed by Experts

**Private cloud** means AI infrastructure on your premises, [managed by an outside provider]{.amber}. You get data control without building the IT team yourself.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**How it works**

- Hardware sits in your data center (or a dedicated facility)
- A vendor (HPE, Dell, IBM) manages the infrastructure
- Your data never leaves your network
- The vendor handles updates, scaling, and maintenance
::::

::::{.column}
**Enterprise examples**

- **DISA** (U.S. DoD): $931M HPE private cloud
- **Barclays**: 50K+ workloads to private cloud
- **2degrees** (NZ): sovereign AI for data residency
::::
:::

Antonio Neri, CEO of HPE: "In the AI era, where data sovereignty, privacy and control of data matter, the industry is seeing a shift back to private clouds." (Neri's company sells private cloud hardware --- but the trend he describes is real.)

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
If mitigations for the evaluation loop were foolproof, this market wouldn't exist. Private cloud exists because [some data cannot risk transit]{.amber} through any external server, even transiently.
:::

## What On-Prem AI Actually Requires {.section-divider}

## More Than a Model

Choosing on-prem means building [an entire AI platform]{.amber}, not just downloading a model.

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**The Model Layer**]{.card-title}

- Open-source LLM (Llama, Qwen, Mistral)
- GPU servers to run inference
- Model serving software (Ollama, vLLM)
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**The API Layer**]{.card-title}

- Internal API endpoint (like OpenAI's, but on your network)
- Authentication, rate limiting, logging
- Lets any internal app call the model programmatically
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**The Interface Layer**]{.card-title}

- Chat UI for non-technical users
- System prompt management
- Conversation history and audit logs
:::
:::

With a cloud provider, you get all three out of the box. On-prem, you build each one.

## The Developer Tooling Gap

For AI-assisted coding, the gap is even wider. A coding agent is [not just an LLM]{.amber} --- it's a harness of tools wrapped around the model.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**What a Coding Agent Needs**]{.card-title}

- File read/write with permission controls
- Terminal execution in a sandbox
- Git integration (commits, diffs, branches)
- Context management (which files to feed the model)
- Multi-agent orchestration
- Memory and session persistence
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**What Cloud Providers Bundle**]{.card-title}

- Anthropic: Claude Code (~500K lines of tooling)
- GitHub: Copilot (IDE-integrated agent)
- Google: Gemini Code Assist
- All include the harness [for free]{.amber} with API access
- Continuously updated by large engineering teams
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
The model is the brain. The harness is everything else. On-prem means building the harness yourself.
:::

## Case Study: The Claude Code Leak

On March 31, 2026, Anthropic accidentally published Claude Code's full source code via an npm packaging error --- making the [architectural complexity]{.amber} of a production coding agent visible for the first time.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**What it revealed**

- ~40 permission-gated tools (file, terminal, git, web, IDE)
- Three-layer memory architecture
- Multi-agent orchestration via prompts
- Granular sandboxing and access control
::::

::::{.column}
**What happened next**

- [claw-code](https://github.com/ultraworkers/claw-code): open-source reimplementation became the fastest-growing repo on GitHub
- Provider-agnostic: works with Claude, OpenAI, or [local models via Ollama]{.amber}
- Other alternatives: Aider, OpenHands, Continue.dev
- All are less mature than the cloud originals
::::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
The leak made clear that a coding agent is not just a model --- it is a [complex harness]{.amber} of tools, permissions, and orchestration that takes significant engineering to build and maintain.
:::

## The Build-vs-Buy Reality

The TCO of on-prem AI is [not just hardware]{.amber}.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Cloud (e.g., Anthropic API)**]{.card-title}

- Model + API + coding agent included
- Pay per token, scale on demand
- No infrastructure team needed
- Continuous model improvements
- Risk: data leaves your network
:::

:::{.card .card-dark}
[**On-Prem**]{.card-title}

- GPU hardware and data center space
- Model serving and API infrastructure
- Build or assemble developer tooling
- Ongoing maintenance and model updates
- Engineering team to support all of the above
- Benefit: [complete data control]{.amber}
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
At low-to-moderate usage, cloud wins on cost. At enterprise scale (thousands of employees, continuous inference), on-prem TCO can be lower --- but only if you amortize the engineering team across enough usage.
:::

## Implement Your Own On-Prem {.section-divider}

To make on-prem concrete, let's start small --- running a model locally on your own machine.

## Ollama: Local LLMs in One Installer

[Ollama](https://ollama.com) lets you run open-source models on your laptop --- no cloud, no API key, no data leaving your machine.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**How it works**

1. Download and run the Ollama installer
2. Run a model: `ollama run gemma4:e2b`
3. Chat locally in the terminal
4. All inference runs on your CPU/GPU
::::

::::{.column}
**Model sizes and RAM**

- **Gemma 4 E2B** (Google, ~2B active) --- runs on [8GB RAM]{.amber}
- **Gemma 4 E4B** (Google, ~4B active) --- runs on 8GB RAM
- **Llama 3.1 8B** (Meta) --- needs 16GB RAM
- Models download once and run offline
::::
:::

## Apple Silicon: A Local AI Advantage

Macs with Apple Silicon have [unified memory]{.amber} --- CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine share one memory pool with no copying bottleneck. This is ideal for LLM inference.

| Mac Configuration | Unified Memory | Largest Model |
|---|---|---|
| MacBook Air / base Pro | 8--16 GB | Gemma 4 E4B, Llama 3.1 8B |
| MacBook Pro (M3/M4 Pro) | 18--48 GB | 30B+ models comfortably |
| MacBook Pro / Mac Studio (M4 Max) | 36--128 GB | 70B models at 30--45 tokens/sec |

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
A 16GB MacBook often outperforms a Windows PC with 32GB for AI workloads, because there is no separate GPU memory bottleneck.
:::

## Exercise: Running a Local Model

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Setup**

1. Go to [ollama.com](https://ollama.com) and download the installer for your OS
2. Run the installer (no terminal needed)
3. Once installed, open a terminal and run:
   ```
   ollama run gemma4:e2b
   ```
4. Then try:
   ```
   ollama run gemma4:e4b
   ```
::::

::::{.column}
**The prompt**

Give each model this prompt and compare their responses:

> *"A private equity firm is evaluating a manufacturing company with $50M in revenue, 12% EBITDA margins, and 8% annual revenue growth. Comparable transactions have been done at 7--9x EBITDA. What is a reasonable valuation range, and what are the top three risks you would flag before proceeding?"*

**What to notice**: quality of reasoning, how each model handles uncertainty, and whether either refuses or hedges differently.
::::
:::

[Both models run entirely on your machine. No data is sent anywhere.]{.amber}

## AI Adoption Roadmap {.section-divider}

## The Sandbox-Audit-Deploy Pattern

Before deploying any AI workflow to your team, follow three phases.

:::{.step-flow}
:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🧪
:::
:::{.step-title}
1. Sandbox
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Test with dummy data
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🔍
:::
:::{.step-title}
2. Audit
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Review logs and logic
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🚀
:::
:::{.step-title}
3. Deploy
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Roll out with monitoring
:::
:::
:::

Test with synthetic data, review logs and logic, then roll out with weekly monitoring.

## Adoption Timeline: From Quick Wins to Strategic

Each adoption stage maps to a trust level.

1. **Quick wins** (weeks) --- *light verification*:
   - Chat for research, summarization, drafts
   - Upload spreadsheets for ad-hoc analysis

2. **Medium-term** (months) --- *test-before-deploy*:
   - Skills for recurring workflows
   - MCP integrations and web apps

3. **Strategic** (quarters) --- *full audit trail*:
   - Production apps with authentication
   - AI usage policy and governance framework

## Summary {.section-divider}

## Summary

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Data Security**]{.card-title}

- Agent evaluation loop
- Schema + synthetic rows
- Mitigations and their limits
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Deployment Spectrum**]{.card-title}

- Public cloud → VPC-hosted → on-prem
- Private cloud and confidential computing
- Build-vs-buy reality
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Enterprise Adoption**]{.card-title}

- Sandbox-audit-deploy
- Quick wins → medium-term → strategic
- Trust is the bottleneck
:::
:::

:::{style="text-align:center; margin-top:1em;"}
[Protect the data, understand the deployment options, adopt in stages.]{.amber}
:::
